channel.title: NYT > Arts > Music
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Kanye West’s Comeback: Sellouts, a Canceled Festival and Big Questions
item.description: Attempting a comeback after years of antisemitic statements, the artist formerly known as Kanye West is facing consequences while trying to navigate a test of his own making.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica

item.title: ‘Innocence’ at the Met Opera Transforms With Backstage Magic
item.description: The set of Kaija Saariaho’s opera “Innocence” transforms with astonishing speed. It takes a big, nimble team to make that possible.
item.dc:creator: Darryn King and Vincent Tullo
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times

item.title: Lady Gaga and Doechii, the Strokes: 8 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Lady Gaga and Doechii strike a pose, the Strokes side-eye consumption and Kelela confronts a crumbling relationship.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:description: The Strokes released their first single from “Reality Awaits,” the band’s first album since 2020, which is due in June and produced by Rick Rubin.

item.title: 5 Classical Music Albums You Can Listen to Right Now
item.description: The latest recording from Pygmalion, Messiaen’s “Quartet for the End of Time” and works written for Anne-Sophie Mutter are among our selections.

item.title: Oasis, Phil Collins and Sade to Join Rock & Roll Hall of Fame
item.description: Billy Idol, Luther Vandross and Wu-Tang Clan will also be inducted, while New Edition, Mariah Carey and Melissa Etheridge failed to make the final cut.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.category: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame and Museum

item.title: 11 Best Coachella 2026 Looks: Justin Justin Bieber, Addison Rae and More
item.description: The fashion at the festival is rarely rooted in reality. That’s what makes it fun.
item.dc:creator: Anthony Rotunno
item.category: Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival
item.media:description: FKA Twigs’s performance attire included a chain-mail hood that she removed to reveal tightly braided red hair.

item.title: South Korea Can’t Build Enough Stadiums to Host Its K-Pop Stars
item.description: South Korea doesn’t have enough arenas to show off its own A-list talent. That’s why BTS began its world tour in a once-neglected, 22-year-old soccer stadium.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:description: Fans gathering outside Goyang Stadium ahead of BTS’s world tour opener in Goyang, South Korea, last week.

item.title: Police Arrest Woman Accused of Stalking Lindsey Buckingham of Fleetwood Mac
item.description: The guitarist, Lindsey Buckingham, had obtained a restraining order against the woman two years ago, but the police said she continued to follow him in March.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Chad Batka for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lindsey Buckingham performing in New York in 2011. A woman who is accused of stalking the former Fleetwood Mac guitarist was arrested over the weekend in Indiana.

item.title: In Minneapolis, Following Prince’s ‘Purple Trail’
item.description: This year is the 10th anniversary of the artist’s death. We made a pilgrimage to the city where he lived and worked.
item.dc:creator: David Farley
item.media:credit: Caroline Yang for The New York Times
item.media:description: For fans of Prince, Minneapolis, the artist’s hometown, is an essential destination. Above, a mural of Prince, painted in 2022 by the street artist Hiero Veiga, in downtown Minneapolis.

item.title: ‘Titaníque’ on Broadway: Wild ‘Titanic’ Parody, With Celine Dion as Kooky Guide
item.description: This “Titanic” parody fueled by Dion’s hits, silly ad-libs and pop culture references had the humblest of beginnings. Now the show has docked on Broadway.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: Sabrina Carpenter Apologizes for Mocking Arabic Call at Coachella
item.description: “I don’t like it,” the pop singer had said from the festival stage, after mistaking a celebratory Middle Eastern cry as a form of yodeling.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.category: Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival
item.media:description: Sabrina Carpenter performing at the Coachella music festival in Indio, Calif., on Friday.

item.title: Jailed for Love Songs? Yes, and Still Singing.
item.description: Nguyen Van Loc was persecuted for performing Hanoi’s old romantic music during (and after) the Vietnam War. He can’t stop. He won’t stop.
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave and Linh Pham
item.media:credit: Linh Pham for The New York Times

item.title: Afrika Bambaataa’s Complicated Legacy
item.description: The tributes to one of hip-hop’s founders were complicated by allegations of sexual abuse made against him in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Guy Trebay
item.media:credit: Lisa Haun/Michael Ochs Archives, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Afrika Bambaataa in a 1986 portrait.

item.title: Review: A George Lewis Premiere at the New York Philharmonic
item.description: George Lewis’s new piece brought in the percussion and piano quartet Yarn/Wire, in an evening that also featured the soprano Golda Schultz.
item.dc:creator: Seth Colter Walls

item.title: Hilde Limondjian, Met Museum’s Longtime Concert Impresario, Dies at 89
item.description: She gave New York debuts to artists like Cecilia Bartoli and Peter Serkin, and introduced new music by Philip Glass and others.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:description: Hilde Limondjian in 2003. Her excellent taste was complemented by her innovative use of the vast resources of the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

item.title: Is a Big Album Dropping? You Might Want to Watch the Road.
item.description: A study found that traffic fatalities increased in the United States by nearly 15 percent on the same days as the biggest album releases.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:description: Researchers from Harvard Medical School studied driver behavior data and found more smartphone use, and a lot more distraction, on days when the likes of Taylor Swift, Bad Bunny and Kendrick Lamar dropped new music.

item.title: Protoje, One of Reggae’s Premier Ambassadors, Doubles Down on His Roots
item.description: The musician’s latest album, “The Art of Acceptance,” features stars of the genre (Damian and Stephen Marley) and fresh updates on nostalgic sounds.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Meschino
item.media:credit: Elliott Verdier for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We started recording the album with the lyrics, ‘Rastafari teach I how to forgive, I’ve been learning how to live and let live,’” Protoje said.

item.title: Afrika Bambaataa, Often Called the ‘Godfather of Hip-Hop,’ Is Dead
item.description: A pioneering rapper and D.J. from the Bronx, he was a key force in propelling hip-hop into the mainstream. He was accused of child sexual abuse later in his career.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams and Hannah Ziegler
item.media:description: Afrika Bambaataa in 2006. Born Lance Taylor, he was hailed as a master of records and drew from a wide range of genres and sources.

item.title: ‘We Are Fierce Competitors’: Live Nation Case Reaches Closing Arguments
item.description: Thirty-four states accused the concert giant of suffocating competition and driving up ticket prices. The company denies being anything but big.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: The Live Nation trial has been closely watched in the music industry as well as in policy circles, where it is a test of the Trump administration’s stance on antitrust enforcement.

item.title: Pink Will Host the 2026 Tony Awards
item.description: Songs by the pop singer-songwriter are part of the Broadway shows “& Juliet” and “Moulin Rouge! The Musical.”
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: Pink, who has won three Grammy Awards, will host the Tony Awards at Radio City Music Hall on June 7.

item.title: A Stroke and a Coma Couldn’t Stop Matt Pinfield, Rock’s Big Booster
item.description: Matt Pinfield, the D.J. and onetime MTV host, returned to the music world months after using up yet another of his “nine lives.”
item.dc:creator: Brian Raftery
item.media:credit: Jamie Lee Taete for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matt Pinfield has been talking about his favorite albums and artists on the air for more than five decades.

item.title: Wayne Perkins, Guitarist to the Stars, Dies at 74
item.description: Mr. Perkins worked with Bob Marley, Joni Mitchell and many others, almost joined the Rolling Stones and turned down an offer from Lynyrd Skynyrd.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: Wayne Perkins in 1972. A versatile guitarist, he could adapt to a wide range of styles, including the melancholy pop of Joni Mitchell’s “Car on a Hill” and Bob Marley’s swaying “Concrete Jungle.”

item.title: Duke Ellington and John Adams: Titans of Classical Americana
item.description: Recent performances by the Orchestra of St. Luke’s and the Philadelphia Orchestra offered music by Duke Ellington and John Adams.
item.dc:creator: Seth Colter Walls

item.title: 5 Minutes That Will Make You Love Detroit Jazz
item.description: The city’s music is spiritual, searching and defiantly local, even when it spreads worldwide. Hear tracks from Yusef Lateef, Geri Allen, James Carter and other Detroit jazz greats.
item.dc:creator: Marcus J. Moore

item.title: Fanciful and Fabulous: ‘Cats: The Jellicle Ball’ on Broadway
item.description: For their 10th life, the cats strut and duckwalk in a reappraisal of Andrew Lloyd Webber’s 1981 musical, which has shifted to the queer ballroom scene.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw and Sara Krulwich

item.title: 9 Ethereal, Emotional, Chaotic and Goofy Cloud-Rap Gems
item.description: A quick guide to the underground genre, featuring fakemink, Bladee, Yung Lean and more.
item.dc:creator: Dani Blum
item.media:description: The British rapper fakemink has staying power.

item.title: Review: ‘Innocence’ Tackles School Shootings at the Met Opera
item.description: Kaija Saariaho’s “Innocence,” a powerful and kaleidoscopic depiction of mass violence and its aftermath, has arrived at the Metropolitan Opera.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Richard Termine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Simon Stone’s realistic production of “Innocence” opened at the Metropolitan Opera on Monday night.

item.title: Joe Conzo Sr., Guardian of Tito Puente’s Legacy, Dies at 83
item.description: He went from being a fan of the “Mambo King” to becoming his manager and personal historian. Later, he kept alive memories of a bygone era of New York Latino culture.
item.dc:creator: Alex Traub

item.title: Wireless Festival Canceled After U.K. Blocks Kanye West From Entering Country
item.description: Entry to the country is being denied to the rapper formerly known as Kanye West because his presence “would not be conducive to the public good.” The festival he was due to headline is now canceled.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Ye, the rapper formerly known as Kanye West, had made a last-minute plea on Tuesday in which he offered to meet with Jews in Britain.

item.title: Irvine Wanted a New Concert Venue. But Not on Live Nation’s Terms.
item.description: As the company’s antitrust trial continues, one incident involving an amphitheater shows how it clashed not with another industry titan, but a small city.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Irvine, a city of 300,000 about an hour’s drive south of downtown Los Angeles, was hoping to work with Live Nation to replace a popular amphitheater that shut down in 2016.

item.title: Is This Bach’s Most Underrated Music?
item.description: The sprawling organ collection “Clavier-Übung III” is not as widely known as it should be. An excellent new recording could change that.
item.dc:creator: David Allen
item.media:credit: Sam Dearden for The New York Times
item.media:description: The English organist James McVinnie said that Bach’s collection “Clavier-Übung III” is still very relevant today.

item.title: How Did Black Music Take Over the World? Let Melvin Gibbs Explain.
item.description: Since the late ’70s, the bassist has worked to map a musical route that mirrored the trans-Atlantic slave trade and birthed nearly all of American popular music.
item.dc:creator: Hank Shteamer
item.category: How Black Music Took Over the World (Book)
item.media:credit: Galen Fletcher for The New York Times
item.media:description: Melvin Gibbs has become one of his generation’s most admired and least easily categorized instrumentalists and conceptualists.
